persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a second A. M. Abbott in British Guiana — a son, brother, or unrelated clerk — since 38 officials share this surname and 'Clerk' is a generic junior title; also the place_inherited check flags that most bio events inherit their colony from the first clause, and edition_cooccurrence mechanically failed. But the match does not rest on the generic 'Clerk' title or on inherited places: the bio explicitly opens 'Clerk to attorney-general, British Guiana, 1871' (place printed, not inherited), and states he became '3rd clerk, immigration department, Oct., 1876' — the staff list shows A. M. Abbott as Third Clerk in the Immigration Department in 1878-1879, an exact position+department+colony+date agreement on a specific office. He then moves to 'stipendiary magistrate's clerk, Mar., 1879', and the staff list correspondingly shifts him to Clerk, Judicial Establishment from 1880 onward, matching the career shape (magistrate's clerk, chief clerk to police magistrate and sheriff, acting magistracies are judicial-establishment posts). Both initials (A and M) match, not a single initial. The edition footprints also overlap heavily (1888, 1889, 1894, 1896, 1898, 1899 in both), contrary to what the mechanical check reported. For a hypothetical second A. M. Abbott to explain this, he would need the same two initials, same colony, same department, same year-range, and a career trajectory tracking the bio's stated promotions clause by clause — not realistic.
persons.would_overturn: A staff list or biography revealing a second 'A. M. Abbott' serving in British Guiana in the 1870s-1890s (e.g., a son with the same initials in the same departments), or a record showing Arthur Moore Abbott left the colonial service or died before 1878 while the staff-list entries continued.

unassigned_stints.reason: Different person: 'Mrs. Maria Abbott', a woman serving as Matron, Female Prison in St Vincent, cannot be the male Arthur Moore Abbott whose entire printed career is in British Guiana; only the weak single-initial M coincides. Also gate-compatible with a biography outside this case.

Different person: 'Miss Abbott' is a woman in the Leeward Islands Treasury and Customs in 1917-1918; no forename, no colony agreement, no position agreement, and the bio's career (starting 1871, last printed 1899) makes a 1917 junior post implausible. Also gate-compatible with a biography outside this case.

Different person: Sir Myles Abbott, Chief Justice of Bermuda 1962-1966. A man whose career began as a clerk in 1871 would be roughly 110 years old; chronologically impossible. The dossier notes this stint is gate-compatible with other biographies including abbott_myles-john_b1906, the obvious match.

notes: The two printed bio versions are the same person (same entry, abbreviated vs expanded typesetting), not duplicate biographies. The mechanical edition_cooccurrence FAIL for the A. M. stint appears to be a checker artifact: bio editions [1888, 1889, 1890, 1894, 1896, 1898, 1899] and stint editions share at least six years. The bio's 'compiler of Summary Conviction Ordinances of the Col., 1887 to 1883' contains an OCR-garbled date range; it was not used as evidence. The staff list continuing to 1900 (beyond the bio's last edition, 1899) is consistent with the biography section simply dropping the entry while he remained in post.
